ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class (FOODON_00002708); no specific spicy ramen subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated spicy ramen code; A00LC (noodle soup dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for spicy ramen as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of spicy ramen weighing approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, broth, protein toppings, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup, restaurant-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; adapted for spicy broth variant
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for spicy ramen exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for restaurant-prepared ramen noodle soup (FDC ID 748967 and related entries), adjusted for a spicy broth variant with chilli oil addition. Sodium is elevated relative to plain ramen due to spicy seasoning paste. Energy and macros are consistent with published restaurant ramen analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Tsuji et al., 2020, Journal of Nutritional Science).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and noodle-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for spicy ramen
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for spicy ramen exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: wheat-based ramen noodles in broth, which the University of Sydney GI Database lists at GI 52–59 (medium). A midpoint of 55 is used. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (55 × 52.0 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 28.6. Carbohydrate per serving derived from per-100g value of 10.4 g × 5 (500 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~2400 mg per 500 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit spicy ramen consumption to occasional servings and request reduced-sodium broth, as a single bowl can provide the full daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 55) and high glycemic load per serving (~28.6) due to refined wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume spicy ramen in moderated portions, pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the medium GI and substantial carbohydrate load per bowl.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from animal-based broth and protein toppings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit spicy ramen frequency due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat, both of which are associated with elevated blood pressure and adverse lipid profiles; choosing leaner protein toppings and consuming only part of the broth can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy ramen is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of springy wheat-based ramen noodles served in a richly seasoned, spicy broth — typically a chicken, pork, or combined stock base infused with chilli paste, chilli oil, miso, or spicy tare (seasoning sauce). The dish is assembled in a deep bowl and topped with ingredients such as chashu pork or chicken slices, a soft-boiled marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), nori (dried seaweed), bamboo shoots (menma), spring onions, and sesame seeds. The broth is the defining element: deeply savoury, umami-rich, and layered with heat from chilli. The noodles are firm and chewy, absorbing the spiced broth as the dish is eaten. Spicy ramen is a popular restaurant and street-food item across Japan and has become widely available in Japanese ramen restaurants globally, including in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 445 kcal, 26 g protein, 15.5 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at approximately 2400 mg per bowl, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 55) and high glycemic load per serving (~28.6) require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard form and contains gluten, egg, and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of spicy ramen?
What are the ingredients in spicy ramen?
What is the glycemic index of ramen?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for spicy ramen was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for restaurant-prepared ramen noodle soup, adjusted for a spicy variant. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based noodles in broth. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is grounded in approved sources and is consistent with published restaurant ramen nutritional analyses.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central ramen entries used as the adaptation basis. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in restaurant ramen per USDA source data. 5. The input record described this food as 'chicken soup' which is an incomplete description; the canonical food name has been corrected to 'Spicy Ramen' reflecting the actual dish. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat noodles in broth; no direct spicy ramen GI measurement was found. 7. Sodium is elevated relative to plain ramen due to spicy seasoning paste and tare; the 480 mg per 100 g figure reflects a composite restaurant-style spicy ramen. 8. The food is served at Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore, per the input source record. 9. Halal status is marked false as standard ramen typically contains pork; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified independently.
